key_insight: Elastic energy reduction can drive abnormal grain growth in ultrafine-grained Ni thin films, selectively favoring grains with in-plane <100> orientation aligned to the loading direction under cyclic elastic loading.
engineering_relevance: This suggests grain stability in thin-film and microstructured metals can be compromised even without plasticity or high temperatures, which matters for reliability under repeated service loads.
method: Phase-field simulations of grain evolution under cyclic elastic loading
Micromechanical analysis linking stress state, grain geometry, and elastic anisotropy
Comparison across systems with varying elastic anisotropy to generalize the mechanism
evidence_signal: The abstract reports simulation-based evidence that the AGG pathway depends on local geometry and stress states and is consistent with elastic-energy minimization as the driving force.
watch_item: The mechanism is established through modeling and micromechanical analysis, so experimental validation across material systems and loading conditions is still needed before broad design use.
paper_title: Phase-field modeling of elastically driven abnormal grain growth
